text:
Title: Weak Presidents in European Parliaments Weak presidents are something that few are aware exist in European politics, let alone have come to define a significant aspect of domestic governance. Generally those that hold presidential office in European countries are directly or at the very least indirectly elected via some manner of democratic vote. Yet regardless of how one goes about gaining presidential office in Europe it has proven the basis of very little in constitutional terms when contrasted with others such as legislative or monarchical leaders. By determining which presidents in Europe's Western states hold strong or weak constitutional authority, and hence differ from either elected prime ministers or appointive monarchs, this work delves into the extent of the variance through an analysis of the constitutional law of Germany and Italy—the strong presidential case—and Austria, Ireland, Finland, and Portugal—the weak presidential case, for the latter countries have "inconsistent" constitutional frameworks for the presidential office, due to their presidential heads of state being elected via popular vote, a method inappropriate to weak presidential offices in constitutional terms.

Beyond the comparative literature on formal presidential powers, this book explores the presidents’ use of informal powers, with a detailed typology and definition of informal authority, and through the examination of presidential activism as informally defined by political elites, citizens and the media. The study utilizes an interactionist approach to describe the conditions under which presidential use of informal authority is effective. Through combining structural elements of the opportunity structure of the presidency with individual aspects of presidential leadership, the book reveals the importance of the opportunity structure, but also concludes that presidential authority is contingent upon the level of public presidential support.
